utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all 5 verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality output overall. Both plans are well-structured and pass all verifier checks, but wi_skills demonstrates greater analytical rigor and executable specificity. Key differentiators: (1) wi_skills explicitly classifies micro-stops as Performance losses and uses utilization ratio alongside OEE for constraint verification, while wo_skills omits loss-type classification; (2) wi_skills includes a concrete upstream buffer/release-rate mechanism to protect the drum (J1.3), which wo_skills entirely omits; (3) wi_skills ties its OEE improvement recommendations to the specific Performance loss category with named components (PN-3841, F-02), whereas wo_skills offers generic preventive maintenance advice. wo_skills performs competently on sequencing, changeover optimization, operator risk, and escalation, but lacks the TOC/DBR buffer logic and targeted loss-category improvement focus that the skill framework provides. Overall, wi_skills is more defensible and executable as a shop-floor plan.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality output overall. While both agents passed all five verifier checks and both addressed frozen-window compliance, changeover-aware sequencing, feasibility arithmetic, and due-date validation, the wi_skills output is more structured and precise. Specifically: (1) wi_skills explicitly frames the campaign consolidation decision with a concrete incremental cost calculation ($30 for 10-min flush vs. a full standalone changeover), whereas wo_skills provides the same conclusion but through a brute-force Python enumeration that is less methodologically transparent in the document itself. (2) wi_skills produces a cleaner, single recommended sequence with full timestamp detail and a dedicated Delivery Impact table, making it easier to audit. (3) wi_skills correctly characterizes the changeover savings narrative (absorbed naturally through Family C queue completion) in a way that directly matches the skill's economic crossover framework. (4) wo_skills, while competent, derives its sequence through computational enumeration rather than principled scheduling logic, and its alternate-option section (prioritizing RUSH-001 first) would increase changeover costs without clear policy justification. Both agents scored identically on J2.5 since the rush order was feasible in both runs, making the customer notification criterion not applicable. Overall, wi_skills demonstrates more defensible, framework-driven reasoning aligned with the skill's intended methodology.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, verifier check V3.5 (escalation with named role and timeframe) failed despite the plan containing named roles and timeframes — this appears to be a verifier regex/format issue rather than a genuine content absence. No agent runtime errors (timeout, crash) were observed in either run.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality plan overall. The key differentiator is J3.3 (capacity haircut application) and J3.5 (overtime business case with dollar quantification): wi_skills correctly applies the 80%/90% capacity reduction factors from the alternate routing table to recalculate completion times, and explicitly cites the $6,200/hour contribution margin to build a quantified OT business case with union compliance notes. The wo_skills plan is competent and passes more verifier checks (5/5 vs 4/5), and correctly handles most structural elements (quarantine exclusion, operator prioritization, 30-min communication deadline, stability lock, named escalation roles), but it treats rerouting at face value without throughput adjustment and recommends OT without any cost justification — exactly the failure modes predicted for a baseline agent. The wi_skills plan is more operationally correct and would be safer to execute on the shop floor.
